data.title: Amazon: Tariff Relief For Now, But Snapback Risk Later
data.description:
It seems that I was right with my thesis on Amazon.com, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMZN), and the panic selloff post Q2 earnings was short-lived. Back then, I said:

I believe the selloff was unwarranted as the retail segment is not showing any signs of slowdown or margin pressure due to the tariffs. As for the margin pressure, I see it as a natural side effect of the investments in data center infrastructure. As long as CapEx is high, OpEx will follow.

Since then, a major event happened that I believe is worth integrating into my thesis. I warn readers that this event is a double-edged sword, which adds a new layer of risks to my bull case. The big event was the extension of the (reciprocal) tariff pause on Chinese imports on August 12. With this extension, the applicable tariff rate is just 10% compared to the 30% that the Trump administration proposed back in May. This is quite bullish for the stock, considering the wide revenue and operating income guidance that management provided in the last earnings call. Overall, I expect results to come in at the higher end of the guidance in Q3 due to this event. However, Q4 could be a rough quarter, especially if the US and China fail to strike a deal. If that happens, I wouldn't be surprised to see a 30% reciprocal tariff on Chinese imports (on top of the Section 301 tariff) taking effect, diluting margins in a key quarter for Amazon due to the holiday season.

In the meantime, I remain bullish, although I may reconsider my rating once the tariff pause comes to an end.

An Uncertain Second Half Of The Year

"It's impossible to know what will happen [with tariffs]" were the words given by CEO Andrew Jassy in his prepared remarks during the Q2 earnings call. I admit I am not particularly excited about that statement. This uncertainty regarding tariffs, particularly in China, brings an overhang to the stock. So far, the Street's estimates for the next four quarters are raw, to say the least.

As seen above, the consensus expects a slight yoy decline in EPS in Q4 this year. In other words, the consensus expects reciprocal tariffs on China to come in higher than the current 10% baseline.

Back in Q2 (prior to the extension of the tariff truce between the US and China), management provided a wide guidance range amid this "dynamic environment". Q3 net sales guidance is expected between $174.0–$179.5B. At the midpoint of that range ($176.75B), we are talking about an 11% yoy change. Not bad, in my view, and quite achievable considering the extension of the pause.

However, the bottom line is where things get interesting. The operating income guidance for Q3 was between $15.5–$20.5B. When compared to the $17.4B in Q3 2024, the yoy change varies between –10.9% to +17.8%, with a midpoint sitting at a modest 3.4%. The comparison looks even worse if I use Q2 as a baseline. In this case, the guidance represents a range between –19.3% and +6.8%, with a midpoint of –6.3%.

That said, this tariff uncertainty is a double-edged sword. On the negative side, if tariffs revert to the May proposed rate (i.e., 30%), then the outlook will likely deteriorate in Q4, especially on the bottom line. However, and this is key, if China and the US strike a deal on trade, and the final tariff is nowhere close to the feared 30% rate, I believe Amazon presents the biggest upside among the Mag 7, considering its exposure to China. According to Morgan Stanley, approximately 25% of the 1-P COGS comes from China.

Furthermore, some sources indicate that over 50% of Amazon's top 3-P sellers are based in China.

I now see two main catalysts for Q4.

The first one is the end of the (reciprocal) tariff pause on Chinese imports into the US. To be precise, the U.S. extension runs to 12:01 a.m. EST on Monday, November 10, 2025. If there’s no deal when the pause ends, the U.S. reciprocal tariff on goods imported from China snaps back to 30% ad valorem, replacing the current +10% that’s in place during the pause. Just to be clear, that 30% is on top of other tariffs, like the 25% from Section 301. So, the effective tariff rate on most products (but not all, e.g., EVs have a 100% Section 301 tariff rate) would be a staggering 55%. At that rate, corporations like Amazon will need to absorb part of the costs, or else they risk a significant contraction in the top-line growth. On the positive side, if the US makes a deal with China, the reciprocal tariff rate could be smaller than initially feared, removing an overhang on the stock.

The second event is related to the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit, which stated at the end of August that the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) does not let the President impose broad, open-ended tariffs on "nearly all goods from nearly every country in the world". On September 9, the Supreme Court agreed to expedite a review, with an oral argument scheduled for November. This is another catalyst to keep a close eye on. That said, in the event that reciprocal tariffs are rolled back, the Trump Administration could use other pathways to implement them back, including a hike under other statutes like Sections 232, 301, or 201. So, don't pop a bottle of champagne if the Supreme Court ruling is unfavorable for reciprocal tariffs.

Power Constraints Slowing Down AWS

As I noted in a recent macro article, titled "How To Time The Peak Of A Bubble", the main limiting factor in the growth of hyperscalers is not cash, but power. From what I'm reading, it seems that AWS is not able to meet demand due to this exact problem:

we have more demand than we have capacity right now. So we could be doing more revenue and helping customers more, and we're working very hard on changing that outcome and how much capacity we have, but it's still like if you look at the business, it's $123 billion annual revenue run rate business and it's still early.

The big question is, how long will it take for the company to resolve this issue?

Well, here is the CEO's reply during the last Q&A:

I don't believe that we will have fully resolved the amount of capacity we need for the amount of demand that we have in a couple of quarters. I think it will take several quarters.

I admit I am not particularly excited about that statement. AWS is the engine of the company in terms of profitability and top-line growth. In the last quarter, AWS was up by 17.5% yoy, surpassing the growth of online stores (11% yoy), 3-P sellers (12% yoy), and only trailing advertising (23% yoy) and other revenue (19% yoy).

Again, I see a double-edged sword here. The power constraint is an overhang in this segment. Therefore, if the company is able to close more power contracts, like the recent 1.9GW nuclear deal with Talen Energy, this overhang could disappear. In other words, the company could return to the growth that we've seen back in 2021.

In the meantime, this remains a drag.

Looking Ahead

On a valuation basis, Amazon has the most attractive P/S multiple in the Mag 7 group (both TTM and FWD basis). Furthermore, on a P/CF basis (my favorite valuation metric), it ranks second only to Meta.

From a price action perspective, the chart below looks quite favorable, with a clear uptrend. Interestingly enough, it seems that the ascending triangle pattern is converging around the next earnings release.

As I noted in this article, I expect Amazon to publish a decent third quarter, mainly due to the extension of the tariff pause on Chinese imports. I believe that could be a catalyst for a breakout, beyond the $240 price mark.

The critical factor to watch is the Q4 guide, especially on the bottom line. Again, I wouldn't be surprised to see a wide range for management's guidance. The other factor to watch closely is the status of AWS’s capacity constraints. If power contracts start flowing through, a major overhang on AWS gets lifted.

data.title: Putting U.K. Equities In Perspective
data.description:
By Tajinder Dhillion, Principal, Fundamental Research | Dewi John, Lipper Research Lead, Europe

UK investors now hold less domestic equity than at any point on record, with foreign ownership dominating UK-listed shares. Over £1.9 trillion has exited UK equities since 2000, as pension funds and wealth managers pivot to global markets - particularly the US.

UK investors hold less UK equities as a proportion of their portfolios than at any point on record, resulting in more than £1.9 trillion being withdrawn from UK-listed companies over the century.

In July, Chancellor Rachel Reeves pulled back from proposals to restrict the amount savers could put into cash Isas, in the hope that this would be diverted to other assets, not least UK equities. This remains a government objective, however, with a Treasury spokesperson commenting: “Our ambition is to ensure people’s hard-earned savings are delivering the best returns and driving more investment into the UK economy.”

Initiatives to promote this include the British Business Bank’s British Growth Partnership, aimed at encouraging more investment from UK pension funds and other institutional investors into the fastest growing and most innovative British companies, and this year’s Mansion House Accord, to encourage UK pension funds to invest more in UK Plc.

This is no easy task. The trend away from domestic equity investment has been underway for decades.

According to the most recent ONS figures, in 1981, UK individuals owned 28.2%, ‘other’ UK holdings, such as pension and insurance funds owned 68.25, while foreign ownership was 3.6%. By 2022, those figures were 10.8%, 31.5% and 57.7%, respectively (chart 1).

Chart 1: Overseas investors dominated ownership of UK quoted shares, 1963 to 2022

Prior to 1963, UK individuals owned most UK shares. Of course, those assets were and are far from uniformly distributed. Share ownership, which had been constant at around 7% of the UK adult population, had trebled to 22% by 1988. By 2002, 30% of people had some form of market-based investment, whether shares, mutual funds or investment trusts. However, by 2022, this had shrunk to 18%, according to research. In addition, many people are members of workplace pension schemes, with 88% (20.8m) of UK-eligible employees covered in 2023.

But, despite this coverage, UK individuals and institutions’ ownership of the UK stock market has fallen from 96.4% in 1981 to 42.3% in 2022. Pension schemes’ exposure to equities has been reduced in part by LDI and accountancy and regulatory changes. As a result, UK pension schemes have reportedly dropped their UK equity exposure from about 50% to 4% over the course of this century.

Going global

Even in the pension world, this may not be fully played out. Scottish Widows, a large UK pension provider owned by Lloyds Banking Group, announced in June 2025 it was reducing its allocation to UK equities in the interest of offering a “more globally diversified approach” to capture “more growth opportunities in high-performing international markets”.

Wealth managers are also following the globalisation trend. The head of manager selection at one large wealth operation told me this was a result of shifting from UK to more global benchmarks, “and this is unlikely to reverse”.

The UK’s shrinking percental of global indices has further squeezed allocations. For example, in December 2008, the US had a weighting of 44.42%, followed by Japan at 10.01%, and then the UK at 8.11% in the FTSE All-World index. In July 2025, those weightings were 63.68%, 5.54% and 3.38%, respectively.

It’s worth adding a codicil here, that global indices are not really a reflection of the global economy. China has a weighting of about 2.7%: lower than Nvidia (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT) and Apple (AAPL). The UK still has a weighting greater than Amazon, if not Apple. China’s GDP is about 17-19% of world GDP – the second largest – and has the largest share of global production: at about 30%, nearly double that of the US. So, both at the stock and country level, there is increasing concentration risk within major global indices. Risk should not be reduced to ‘active bets’.

Finding value

Do we see a similar exodus with UK mutual fund and ETF investors? We have looked at how UK investors’ allocations between UK, US and global equity funds (summing flows and net assets for these national classifications and their Income variants) have changed over the past two decades.

In 2004, UK investors held £116.96bn in Equity UK and Equity UK Income funds, compared to £18.99bn in their global equivalents, and £14bn in US. UK equities were therefore 78% of the total. In 2024, that figure was 27.3%. The largest change was between 2019 and 2020, when the Covid crisis broke, where UK equity total net assets fell from 53.2% to 43.9%.

Chart 2: Global, UK and US equity total net assets (%)

That’s reflected in flows to these classifications (chart 3). Between 2004 and July 2025, global funds attracted £102.62bn, US funds £31.65bn, while their UK equivalents have seen outflows of £101.99bn. That is heavily skewed to the period from 2021, where UK funds shed £95.62bn—again, reflected on the jump in relative assets in chart 2.

Chart 3: Global, UK and US equity fund flows (GBP bn)

The UK, which is a value-tilted market, has suffered from the outperformance of tech stocks as equity markets rebounded from the global financial crisis, and investors piled first into the FANGS pre-Covid, then into the Magnificent Seven. This is what has caused the US as a proportion of global indices to balloon.

Using LSEG Datastream, we illustrate the value-tilt in the UK through the FTSE 350 index (chart 4), where Financials, Consumer Staples, and Industrials make up over half of the index. Technology has one of the lowest weightings – less than 4% – and has just about managed to double its weight over the past 15 years. This limited exposure combined with modest growth in weight has contributed to UK investors going global to access large-cap growth stocks.

The Russell 1000 is the leading marketplace for investors seeking growth exposure. Technology holds the largest weighting, representing more than one-third of the index. Separately, the Magnificent Seven’s combined weight is near an all-time high at 32.4%, having quadrupled its weight over the past decade. Furthermore, to illustrate the growth nature of these companies, the group’s weight within the Russell 1000 Growth index has reached a record 61.9%.

Chart 4: Index Composition Weights for FTSE 350 and Russell 1000

While UK equity funds have outperformed year-to-date and in 2022, they have underperformed US equity funds over 17 of the past 20 full years, and 12 out of 20 versus global funds (chart 5). The correlation of US and global returns will have increased along with the former’s weight in global indices.

Chart 5: Equity Global, UK and US annualised performance, 2005-July 20025 (%)

Over the past two decades, that has made a significant difference (chart 6). Clearly, a “more globally diversified approach” to capture “more growth opportunities” has paid off, particularly in relation to the US from 2012.

The Russell 1000 has outperformed the FTSE All World and FTSE 350 indices by 201 and 331 basis points, respectively, over the past three decades. In the past ten years, this spread has widened further to 264 and 651 basis points, with the Russell 1000 delivering an annualised total return of 13.6% compared to 11.0% and 7.1%, respectively.

Chart 6: Equity Global, UK and US cumulative performance, July 2005-July 2025 (%)

This outperformance helps explain the valuation premium that US equities command over the rest of the world. The forward P/E of the Russell 1000 is about 22.5x (~20.2x ex. Mag-7), a level last seen in late 2021 and placing it in the 96th percentile over the past 20 years. With a forward P/E of 12.7x for the FTSE All Share index, the relative valuation premium of US equities has been rising steadily since 2016, peaking at nearly 2x last year before falling to 1.74x, reflecting UK outperformance year-to-date (Chart 7).

Although the UK market offers investors a higher dividend yield of 3.3% compared with only 1.2% in US equities, income is just one part of the total return equation. Ultimately, the trajectory of earnings growth and profitability remain key drivers of equity performance, and this currently favours US markets.

Earnings expectations are high for US large-cap (growth) when viewed through the lens of the StarMine Intrinsic Value (IV) model, which shows a market-implied 5-year EPS CAGR of 12.0% for the Russell 1000 – well above its forward 5-year EPS CAGR of 8.7% (table 8). This difference widens even further when looking at Russell 1000 Growth, with a market-implied CAGR of 20.1% vs. a forward EPS CAGR of 11.1%. Growth expectations are more balanced in US small-caps. Put simply, excluding small-caps, today’s prices imply investors expect much faster earnings growth than what forward estimates suggest, as the market-implied rate effectively backfills the level of growth needed to justify current valuations.

By contrast, we see the opposite dynamic in the UK, with markets pricing in significantly less growth today than what forward estimates imply, signalling that UK equities may be undervalued relative to their fundamentals. We infer the same conclusion when looking at the aggregate StarMine Relative Value (RV) model in table 8, which shows an aggregate RV score of 63 for the FTSE 100 compared to a score of 29 for the Russell 1000 (higher scores indicate ‘inexpensive’).

Looking at the second part of the total return equation, net profit margins for the Russell 1000 are at a record 12.9% compared with 10.4% for the FTSE All Share. Looking ahead, the Mag-7 are expected to deliver aggregate net profit margins of 26.5% in 2026, alongside revenue and earnings growth forecasts of 12.3% and 15.9%, respectively. The group continues to surpass market expectations, irrespective of the high bar set by analysts. In the latest earnings season (Q2), the group saw aggregate earnings growth of 27.3% and revenue growth of 15.5%, marking nine consecutive quarters of double-digit growth and underscoring their resilience regardless of the headwinds that come their way.

Chart 7: Relative Valuation, US and UK Forward 12m P/E

Table 1: StarMine Aggregate Scores

Are we at a turning point? After all, in the words of Herb Stein, if something can’t go on forever, at some point it will stop. UK equity valuations are relatively attractive, and YTD performance has been relatively good—albeit, in an environment where market leadership changes frequently. While the structural headwinds for increasing UK equity allocation remain, it’s also possible that in a market so heavily sold, initiatives such as the British Growth Partnership and the Mansion House Accord might feed enough capital to companies to support valuations, helping foster a positive feedback loop between performance and flows.

There are, of course, many “what ifs?” here. When it comes to identifying such a turning point, that ever-irritating question — “are we there yet?” — remains devilishly tricky to answer.

data.title: McDonald's: A Solid Defensive Play, But Unlikely To Outperform The Index
data.description:
McDonald's (NYSE:MCD) is one of the largest restaurant chains around the globe. The company owns and franchises restaurants globally and offers iconic food items, such as burgers and French fries.

MCD is known as one of the safest dividend-paying consumer discretionary names. Given its defensive approach, the company is an appealing diversification to investors who favor income and seek to withstand market turbulence with lower volatility.

At a forward P/E of roughly 25, the company appears to be trading at elevated levels. MCD faces significant growth headwinds, but its iconic brand is one of the main reasons why investors are willing to pay a premium. The company is part of the S&P500 Dividend Aristocrat Index, which partly justifies trading at elevated levels, but high valuations indicate upside may be limited.

I think that McDonald's is a hold with a $329 price target over the next 12 months, implying 7% upside and broader market underperformance. I believe MCD remains a safe dividend selection for investors preferring stability and steady income, but with substantial top-line headwinds ahead, I don't see a catalyst that could boost the stock higher.

Q2 2025: The Restaurant Giant Is Steadily Growing

On August 6, McDonald's reported Q2 2025 results. This was another strong quarter for the company, with expectations exceeded on both top and bottom lines. The report highlighted that the restaurant giant continues to grow, mainly driven by international markets.

MCD reported $6.84 billion in consolidated revenue, underscoring 5% year-over-year growth. Management pointed out that the US market grew 2.5% on a comparable sales level year-over-year, while international operated markets saw a 4% increase, and international developmental licensed markets grew the fastest, rising by 5.6%.

The company posted $3.23 billion in operating income, which represented substantial 11% growth year-over-year. However, management noted that excluding restructuring charges, the company achieved roughly 7% growth compared to the same quarter last year. This has resulted in diluted EPS of $3.14, reflecting a 12% increase. The net income rose 11% year-over-year to $2.25 billion.

I think it is extremely impressive that a company with such a long history proves over and over again that it is able to reinvent its business and continue growing its revenue. This is a great example of a well-executed company that is highly efficient and extremely profitable, and that is able to grow its bottom line by double digits.

This was a strong quarter for the company. Despite slower growth in the US, MCD sees increasing demand globally, which could continue driving its revenue growth. However, at the current valuation, I don't think this is enough for a significant multiple expansion, but the stock price should follow the company's bottom-line growth.

MCD Appears To Be Elevated, But It Deserves A Premium Valuation

McDonald's trades at a forward P/E of 25.33, nearly 39% higher than the industry's average of 18.21. This is significantly elevated, and is also roughly 9% above the average of the S&P500 of 23.23. Based purely on the bottom-line and multiple expansion, MCD appears to be traded at a fair price, considering double-digit EPS growth, but the revenue is below the market-average of double-digits, and top-line increase due to its already significant moat may be limited.

The current P/E is at the higher range of its typical trading channel. Even though upside is always possible, I think it is more likely the company will consolidate over the next few years, which could result in appreciation in the stock price, but broader market underperformance.

On a PEG (non-GAAP forward) basis at 3.40, roughly a 90% premium to peers' average of 1.79, indicates that the company is expensive and downside potential is a risk. On a forward EV/Sales at 10.37, the company appears approximately 7x overvalued versus the sector median of 1.34, suggesting a lot of further growth may already be priced in. I understand that a premium brand deserves a higher premium, but from a valuation perspective, MCD needs a catalyst for continued multiple expansion.

At a forward EV/EBITDA of 18.85, the company appears 70% overvalued versus the industry's average of 11.09. Price/sales on a forward basis at 8.35 compared to peers at 0.97, an 8x premium, suggesting downside risk and risk of multiple contraction.

The company is heavily leveraged with $55.89 billion in total obligations. This is a significant financial burden, which affects bottom-line growth. On the upside, the Fed is expected to lower interest rates, which could result in substantially lower interest expense, justifying continued EPS expansion.

Last quarter, MCD paid $390 million in interest expense; thus, a more favorable interest-rate environment could give an opportunity to refinance debt.

McDonald's grew its revenue by only 1.17% over the past 12 months, indicating the company is facing growth headwinds. This is another argument for a hold stance, considering it lacks a catalyst for further upside.

The EBITDA forward growth rate of 3.95% is almost in line with the sector median of 4.16%, implying a 4% difference. The EBIT forward growth rate of 3.77% is roughly 18% below the peers' average of 4.62%.

MCD grew its bottom line by 2.17% over the past year, which is higher than the industry's average of 0.32%, but insignificant enough for broader market outperformance. The forward diluted EPS growth rate of 3.79% is nearly 40% below the sector median of 6.21%, supporting the consolidation stance.

On the upside, the free cash flow per share growth rate of 6.25% is roughly 2.3x higher than peers at 2.79%. The operating cash flow grew by 2.05% over the past 12 months, while the industry's average contracted by 1.50%. McDonald's is a high-quality business, which, despite growth headwinds, continues to expand on both top and bottom lines.

The company has a significant dividend history — over the past 48 years, MCD has never missed a dividend payment and has continuously grown its payout to shareholders. McDonald's offers a dividend yield of 2.31%, which is a market-average yield, which is an advantage for investors who favor income from their positions.

The 1-year dividend growth rate (TTM) of 6.89% is nearly 55% higher compared to the industry's average of 4.44%. The dividend growth rate 10-year (CAGR) of 7.61% is in line with the sector median of 6.98%. The company continues its shareholder-friendly capital approach.

The cash dividend payout ratio (TTM) of 72.41% appears to be significantly higher than that of peers, at 35.30%, more than 2x underperformance. This is an area to monitor for investors, as an elevated level could indicate risks tied to continued growth or payout. However, I think its shareholder-friendly capital approach should remain one of management's main focus areas.

McDonald's is highly profitable and operates at above-market margins, which justifies the company trading at a premium. The gross profit margin (TTM) of 56.99% is roughly 47% higher than the industry's average of 38.67%. The company operates at the net margin (TTM) of 32%, which is 6.5x higher than the sector median of 4.32%. This supports a bullish stance for the company and justifies its trading at a premium further.

The company has delivered a 14.97% return on total capital (TTM), roughly 2.4x compared to peers' 6.11%. MCD achieved a 14.09% return on total assets (TTM), 3.7x advantage versus the sector median of 3.74%. McDonald's earned $9.7 billion in cash from operations (TTM), while its competition earned only $295 million.

McDonald's is a phenomenal company that operates at above-market margins and is highly profitable. The company is able to steadily grow its top and bottom lines while applying a shareholder-friendly capital approach with dividends. I think it is an iconic company that is well-run, but for continued multiple expansions, it would need a catalyst, which I can't see at the moment. I believe it is likely the company will consolidate, with slight appreciation due to bottom-line expansion.

Wall Street analysts forecast $13.35 in EPS for FY2026. This would imply 8.27% bottom-line growth. I think this is likely, considering the company is highly profitable with growing operating cash flow.

If we apply the current earnings multiple of 25x, which I think is reasonable considering a high-quality brand and steady growth, to FY2026 EPS of $13.35, we come to my price target of $329. This implies 8% upside for the company, but not the outperformance of the broader market. Given its defensive approach, I think demand for the stock will remain, as investors may be willing to diversify after the market rallied from April lows without any significant pullback.

Main Risks And Concerns

McDonald's faces several risks and concerns that could significantly impact the stock price in the near term.

MCD is growing its top-line steadily, but slowly. This is a risk, as weaker consumer sentiment or macroeconomic uncertainties could raise a stagnation potential, which could initiate the selloff. Despite McDonald's being favored given its defensive approach, a short-term pullback is likely alongside the broader market.

The company trades at an elevated level, and uncertainties could result in multiple contractions. Considering its growth, the company is trading at a premium, and any weaker guidance or worse-than-anticipated results could result in a sharp correction.

McDonald's is facing fierce competition. There are many fast food restaurant rivals, such as Burger King or Taco Bell, fast-casual chains, such as Chipotle, and delivery companies, like Uber and DoorDash, which are operating in a saturated market and fighting for market share. There is always a risk consumers may choose an alternative, but on the other hand, MCD's strong history suggests McDonald's is an iconic brand, and people just enjoy their meals.

If none of the above risks and concerns materialize, MCD is likely to experience upside. However, I think that without a catalyst, upside is limited, and a compelling dividend yield with a defensive approach should protect the downside.

Conclusion: MCD Is Iconic, Great Defensive Play, But Without Significant Growth, It Shouldn't Outperform The Broader Market

I think McDonald's is an incredible company, a textbook business, which offers a defensive approach for investors who favor diversification in a high-quality company with an appealing dividend yield. It has been a safe and steadily appreciating investment, but struggled to outperform the broader market for years.

I believe it is likely that the trend will remain. In order for a significant upside, MCD needs a growth catalyst, which I struggle to see. The stock should follow bottom-line expansion, but I think the upside is limited. An attractive dividend yield, iconic brand, and a high-quality business model offer downside protection. It is an appealing defensive play that could help withstand market turbulence, but faces growth headwinds that limit its potential.

McDonald's is a hold for me with a $329 price target over the next 12 months, implying 7% upside, but broader market underperformance.

35 Wall Street analysts predict a $332 price target over the next year with a roughly 8% increase. This comes in line with my forecast, but time will tell if MCD turns out to be a better or worse investment than analysts anticipated.

data.title: Melt-Up Ahead?
data.description:
If you had told anyone following financial market trends on April 2 this year that by September just about any asset class under the sun would be basking in positive returns for the year, they would have called you crazy. But the doom and gloom of the “liberation day” tariff announcement quickly dissipated, and since then, the markets have learned to take each and every curve ball thrown their way in stride, shrugging off any longer-term implications while reveling in the joys of a short-term sugar rush. Everything from gold to emerging market and European equities to our own S&P 500 is up double digits, and even the once-jittery bond market has settled into a comfortable place with prices up low to mid-single digits across a spectrum of credit risk. It’s a year for the Pollyannas of the world. And that sugar rush may have a ways to go before it ends.

Party Like It’s 2021

It wasn’t all that long ago that we had a similar dynamic. 2021 was the year of the “everything rally,” the year when the punters of the world united to pump up the prices of dubious stocks like GameStop while loading up on all things crypto and outbidding each other for “unique” digital images of primates in various postures of ennui. The fun back then ended, of course, when the Fed took away the punchbowl of zero-level interest rates and began its extensive monetary tightening program in early 2022.

Interest rates are still high relative to where they were in 2021, but that may be about to change in a way that gives this year’s everything-goes rally another burst of upward movement. The Federal Open Market Committee will meet next week, and investors are all but certain that, come 2:00 pm Eastern time next Wednesday, the Fed funds target rate will drop by 0.25 percent. The FOMC has three more meetings this year (including next week’s), and prediction markets have pegged a rate cut for each of those meetings, having the Fed funds rate end the year 0.75 percent lower than where it is today. Couple that with the frequent impulse for markets to rise during the holiday season, and it’s not hard to see why pundits from a number of corners are furiously raising their estimates for risk-asset performance between now and New Year’s Eve.

Reality Bites Back (Sometime, Probably)

A rationally-minded person might ask why all this good cheer is happening at a time when there doesn’t seem to be much in the way of good news out there. The labor market is in a clear slowdown phase, and this week we learned that the BLS jobs numbers reported for the twelve months between March 2024 and March 2025 were revised down by 911,000 – that’s roughly 75,000 fewer payroll gains over that period than previously reported. Also this week, we saw that inflation is not showing much sign of trending down. Year-on-year core inflation (measured by the Consumer Price Index) is up 3.1 percent. Grocery prices, which don’t show up in the core inflation number but which very much do show up in household budgets, rose by 0.6 percent in the month of August alone. The effect of tariffs as a sales tax on consumers is showing up in an ever-widening array of goods and services.

The stickiness of inflation complicates the Fed’s interest rate calculus, but the central bank has been clear in recent messaging that current concerns are tilted towards the jobs side of its dual mandate. So while the market is probably right that rate cuts are coming, they will be coming with plenty of attendant concerns about future developments. Several FOMC members have expressed confidence that inflation will level off and subside after one or two months of tariff-related increases, so job number one is making sure that employment conditions don’t venture far enough south to push the economy into recession territory. Maybe they’re right – and we can see a world in which markets continue to buy into this worldview for some time to come. But reality has a way of biting back, sooner or later. In our view, this is a time neither for overly defensive pessimism nor excessive optimism.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Boston Beer Is A Reasonably Priced Growth Stock
data.description:
The Boston Beer Company (NYSE:SAM) has dropped in price over the past year and especially over the past five years, and its price has increased only 2% over the past decade. While the company has struggled recently, it is a growth stock. In this piece, I explain what impacted the company, why its outlook is very positive and why I find it to be attractively priced.

What The Boston Beer Company Does

Founded in 1984, the Boston-based company went public in late 1995. Founder Jim Koch, who became CEO again this summer, moved the company beyond beer in 1997 with Hardcore Cider. The company sells a lot of beer, but it does other beverages too. The company operates in one segment, and its filings and news releases don't break out beer sales or other beverages.

The company's website lists 5 brands on its "About Us" page, including beer brands Samuel Adams and Dogfish Head. It also mentions Truly (seltzer that is 5% alcohol, launched in 2016), Twisted Tea (a hard tea drink, launched in 2001) and Angry Orchard (hard cider, launched in 1997). While I don't like that the company does not break down sales by category, I do like that it has moved beyond beer.

Founder Jim Koch serves as CEO and owns a lot of the stock (20% as of 12/31/24)). CFO Diego Reynoso joined the company two years ago and worked previously at Tyson Foods, Constellation Brands and Beam Suntory.

In H1 of this year, the company generated revenue of $1.04 billion, up 4% from the first half of 2024. Gross margin improved from 45.0% to 49.1%. SG&A was boosted by higher advertising spending, growing 8%. Operating income expanded by 34%. EPS, reported at $7.58, grew 40%. For H1, the company generated $128.4 million of operating cash flow and spent $24.2 million on capital expenditures. So, SAM was growing and generating free cash flow.

I am primarily a cannabis industry analyst, but my interest in SAM has little to do with their involvement in the cannabis industry, which is restricted to Canadian beverages. In their 10-K, the word "cannabis" appeared a dozen times. The company in 2021 set up a subsidiary to develop non-alcoholic cannabis beverages, and it first sold Emerald Hour products in 2024. The company does not disclose sales levels of this product, and it has no plans to produce or sell products outside of Canada at this time. In the U.S., cannabis is more of a threat than an opportunity currently.

How the Pandemic Impacted Boston Beer

I first noticed this stock in 2020 when it soared during the pandemic. This move was driven by rapidly growing sales of Truly. I noticed how crowded my grocery store was with those seltzers.

During the pandemic, people were at home, were worried, and could not spend money in a lot of ways to which they had become accustomed. It's not surprising that people drank more alcoholic beverages, including these seltzers. The stock shot up a lot early on in 2020 after the pandemic hit, and it went up throughout the year. It gained 163.1% in 2020, hitting an all-time high near $1350 in April.

Again, I follow the cannabis industry closely, and it saw big demand during the early days of the pandemic too, as did other alcohol companies.

A More Recent Challenge

A move away from drinking alcohol may be weighing on the stock, but typically in weak economies, people do drink more. The economy does appear to be weakening!

More recently, the tariffs have an impact on the company. The company reports that 6% of its revenue is from outside of the U.S. (mainly Canada). In its Q2 press release, the company guided to a total cost of $15-20 million for 2025, which was down from its prior guidance. It estimates that the EPS hit will be $0.96 to $1.28. In Q2, the impact was $3.0 million.

The Boston Beer Outlook Is Promising

SAM has ten analysts covering the company, and, according to Koyfin, the analysts expect revenue to decline in 2025 by 2% to $1.97 billion and then to increase by 1.5% in 2026 to $2.00 billion. EBITDA is forecast to decline 5% in 2025 to $232.7 million and then climb 12% to $260.5 million in 2026. EPS are projected to drop 5% in 2025 to $8.99 and then rise 20% in 2026 to $10.75. The current 2027 projection is $12.41, so the EPS are expanding, though earnings are down a lot from 2021.

SAM Is Cheap

The company has 8.82 million Class A shares and 2.07 million Class B shares as of 7/18. The B shares, owned by CEO and Chairman Koch, are convertible into Class A shares and have voting rights. At the end of Q2, there were 287K options outstanding, but the weighted average exercise price was $327.35. I exclude the options from the fully-diluted share-count, but I add 225K RSUs outstanding to get 11.12 million shares. At $220, this gives SAM a market cap of $2.45 billion. The company has been repurchasing its stock, and spent $99.1 million in H1, paying $236.70 per share on average. On 8/11, it entered into a 10b5-1 plan to repurchase up to $50 million in Q4, which is what they had done for Q3 (announced by SEC filing in May).

The balance sheet at the end of Q2 was very strong, with no debt and total liabilities of $356 million. The ratio of current assets to current liabilities was a healthy 1.8X, with cash of $212 million. At $220, the enterprise value is $2.23 billion.

The company ended Q2 with total equity of $912 million, so the price/book is 2.7X. Reducing the equity for intangibles and goodwill, leaves the price to tangible book value at 3.1X, which seems pretty low for a debt-free and profitable company and offers some downside protection.

The stock currently trades at 25 PE for 2025, but a PE for 2026 of 20.5X. Using enterprise value to projected EBITDA, the stock trades at 8.6X projected EBITDA for 2026. These metrics seem low compared to the history over the past decade:

Compared to peers Anheuser-Busch InBev (BUD), Constellation Brands (STZ) and Diageo (DEO), all of which are larger in market cap, the stock seems attractive. BUD, with a market cap of $114 billion, trades at 15X PE and 9X EV EBITDA. It has a lot of debt. Constellation Brands has a market cap near $25 billion. Its PE is 12X, and its EV/EBITDA is 10.5X. Diageo has a market cap of $56 billion and trades with a forward PE of 15X and an EV/EBITDA of 12X. These three stocks do pay a dividend, while SAM does not. All three of them have negative tangible book value, so SAM looks better protected to the downside.

It Is a Potential Acquisition Target

SAM has been involved with potential acquisitions already, and I think that the stock is cheap enough and the company good enough that it could eventually take place, though my interest in the stock isn't based on this.

Ben Kovler, Founder and Chairman and CEO of Green Thumb Industries (OTCQX:GTBIF), a leading MSO in the cannabis space, sent a letter to Jim Koch in June 2024 suggesting that the two companies merge. The Wall Street Journal published a story about it on June 4th, which was after it had reported a potential acquisition by Suntory that was reported on 5/31. Suntory is interesting, as Kovler's father sold Jim Beam to the company, and SAM's CFO used to work there in 2016 and 2017.

Here is the letter from Kovler to Koch:

I was amused by the GTI inquiry, as I understood how difficult it would be for the NYSE-listed SAM to become an OTC company. I do like the idea a lot of a leading cannabis company merging with a leading alcohol company, but the timing was not and is not right. Both stocks are down a lot since then. Suntory also revealed just before the GTI news that it was not in discussions. I actually owned the stock and sold ahead of this as it had rallied above $300.

There are other companies that could be interested in SAM in my view. Heineken (OTCQX:HEINY) and Molson Coors (TAP) were mentioned in 2024 ahead of the Suntory rumor. TAP has a market cap of $9.6 billion with debt of $6.5 billion, and the stock trades at just 8.7 PE, so it doesn't seem that likely to me. HEINY is bigger and trades at a higher PE, but below SAM. Again, I am not counting on M&A.

Koch became CEO again in August, replacing Michael Spillane, who had been named CEO in April of 2024. Spillane resigned for personal reasons. Koch served previously as CEO from 1984 to 2001. On the last conference call, which preceded this change, Koch spoke and answered several questions. I like that the person running the company owns so much stock and am confident in him that he will look for a good merger if he pursues that strategy.

The SAM Chart Is Promising

While SAM is down in 2025 and down a lot since it peaked in 2021, it has done very well since its IPO, rising 675% in 30 years:

Over the past 10 years, the stock is up only 0.5%, but the growth since the IPO 30 years ago has been 7.1% annualized.

Looking at the more recent action, the stock is trading above the 185.34 low that was set in June, but it is way down from the 395 area in September 2023. There have been a couple of gaps down in trading:

Conclusion

I follow about 90 stocks for personal investment, and SAM is my best idea for GARP investors. I don't own it now, but I have owned it off and on over the past 18 months. I am very bearish on stocks right now. My wife does hold it in her IRA.

The company seems to be growing again after growing too fast during the pandemic and then shrinking. The company has a strong balance sheet, and the stock seems cheap.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best GARP Idea investment competition, which runs through September 23. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: The North West Company: Defensive By Nature, Challenged By Fire And Fiscal Tightening
data.description:
In my last piece on North West Company (TSX:NWC:CA) - a rather unconventional retailer-and-service provider operating mainly in remote communities across Northern and Western Canada, as well as the Caribbean and the Virgin Islands - I landed on a ‘Hold,’ with a price target somewhere in the mid-$40s.

In some ways, the stock still works as a decent Defensive Pick. It throws off a low-to-mid single-digit yield, carries little debt (Net Debt/EBITDA at 0.75x), and generates solid FCF.

But to me, it still feels like the underdog compared to other Grocery/Supermarket picks that check all the same boxes but also come with stronger growth drivers and less macro baggage.

At the end of the day, stability is what you want in this kind of stock. And since a big chunk of North West Company demand is tied to government transfer programs, that stability can start to look a bit shaky.

Since my last article a little under two months ago, the stock is down just over 1%, while the S&P gained a bit more than 3% in the same period.

The stock was sitting a little over $50 before Q2 earnings, which pretty much tells you the market didn't love what it saw.

I think the headwinds (wildfires in Canada, smaller social benefit transfers, and a softer Caribbean market) set the tone more than the positives like the Next 100 program or the private label test.

Fires and Cuts: How Do They Impact Sales?

Back in July, I flagged two major headwinds I expected to weigh on Q2 and beyond:

  1. Wildfires in Manitoba, Ontario and Saskatchewan (14 million acres burned, 500 structures destroyed and 39,000 people evacuated), affecting ~10% of stores near the affected areas;
  2. Cuts to government benefits — from SNAP reductions to federal subsidy freezes. According to Brookings and Alaska Beacon, about 60% of tribes are dealing with frozen benefits, and roughly a quarter have seen them cut altogether.

But let's dig a little deeper here.

Starting with the fires, the CEO mentioned that six more stores than initially estimated were hit, bringing the total to 16 locations affected by the wildfires.

And, despite a sequential reduction in August (primarily in Ontario, with only three locations completely evacuated and others partially), they remain strong in other regions in Saskatchewan, Northwest Territories, and Manitoba (areas with a high concentration of North West Company brands like Giant Tiger, Northern Store, and NorthMart).

I also think that soaring energy costs (2 to 6 times the national average in some regions) made worse by damaged hydroelectric lines, are increasing reliance on diesel and putting a bit of a damper on both discretionary and essential spending.

So basically, it's not just the fires themselves, but their aftermath that could keep putting pressure on The North West Company.

Looking at income transfer programs, there are two that overlap the most with North West Company’s customer base:

  • Jordan's Principle: This program focuses on the monetary and service needs of First Nations children. Demand has grown steadily since 2016, and especially after 2021. However, in February of this year, the federal government made some adjustments to eligibility, making them more restrictive. To give you an idea, the projection for 2026 is that they will be ~$780 million (well below the ~$1.6 billion in 2024).

  • Inuit Child First Initiative, also known as ICFI: This is essentially very similar to Jordan's Principle, but specifically targeted at Inuit children. The most important aspect for The North West Company is the food vouchers, which were extended until the end of March next year, creating some uncertainty about whether they will continue after that. In April, like Jordan's, the ICFI also talked about some changes, making them more restrictive ($167.5 million in 2023-2024, decreasing by 27.3% to $121.7 million for 2025-2026).

Interestingly, both programs were in full swing around 2021, a period during which the company reported double-digit growth in revenue and EBITDA. This is a stark contrast to the slow pace the company has seen over the past five years, with revenue averaging just 4% and EBITDA around 2.7%.

Taking a Look at Q2 FY 2025

Looking at the quarterly results, we can easily see why I called the company 'boring' in my last write-up.

Sales came in at $647 million in Q2—basically flat compared to last year. Considering the headwinds I mentioned, that's not terrible, but it's a clear slowdown from the 3.8% growth we saw in Q1, keeping the deceleration trend going.

Just take a closer look:

There was a 1.8% year-over-year decline in same-store sales in Canada (underperforming the system, which saw same-store sales decline 1.1% year-over-year), offset by stronger sales at NorthMart Pharmacy and air cargo.

Gross margin held steady at 33.9%, basically the same as last year, with just a tiny 0.1% uptick. In other words, whatever boost the 'Next 100' is bringing is being offset, at least for now, by customers spending a bit less per visit.

On the bright side, private labels are rolling out by the end of October—so we could see a nice bump after that.

Due to slightly lower SG&A expenses year over year, EBIT was $55.2 million, an increase of just 0.6% year over year. Net income saw slightly higher growth (+2.2% year over year), landing at $36.1 million, benefiting from lower interest on debt.

Looking at cash flow, they reported OCF of $44.1 million. Due to Capex of $30.8 million, FCF was $13.3 million—slightly exceeding the $13.2 million in the same period last year.

They also issued $15.9 million in net debt, allowing them to continue returning capital to shareholders. Dividends temporarily exceeded OCF ($19.2 million), and they also returned $4.5 million in buybacks, nothing significant.

And no, even though distributions exceeded OCF, I don't see a problem here, since:

  1. Operating asset headwinds reduced OCF by nearly $20 million in the quarter;
  2. They are sitting on $68.5 million in cash. This cushion could protect these returns during periods when OCF is weaker;
  3. Even with net debt in Q2 sitting at $315.4 million in long-term debt, the company still has plenty of breathing room. Debt/EBITDA is right around 1x on a TTM basis, so they could easily take on more if needed.

That said, I continue to project a low single-digit shareholder yield year-over-year even if they slightly accelerate buybacks in H2 FY 2025.

Is North West Company Still 'Hold'?

Since the outlook hasn't really changed much—headwinds still eating into the benefits of the 'Next 100,' tourism in Alaska staying weak, and the South Pacific remaining soft—I'd say my earlier predictions still hold up.

In this case, based on a projected EBITDA of ~$299 million, we would be looking at an EV/EBITDA multiple of 8.8x.

This is a premium compared to peers like Empire Company (OTCPK:EMLAF) (7.5x), SpartanNash (SPTN) (6.9x), and Parkland (PKI:CA) (6.9x). Compared to multinationals like Ahold Delhaize (OTCQX:ADRNY) (6.3x) and Tesco (OTCPK:TSCDY) (7.9x), this stock also appears stretched.

Now, I agree that we should consider the consistency of dividends paid (in the case of North West Company, it has 27 years of consecutive payments), but many names on this list have a high single-digit shareholder yield, easily surpassing North West.

I also think it deserves to trade at a discount to peers like Empire and Parkland, considering its exposure to macro and natural risks—and the fact that it's only now starting to roll out private labels.

And, in fact, it seems the market has been considering this discount for the last five years. Historically, North West is trading at stretched multiples, unlike Parkland, for example.

That said, my rating here remains 'Hold.' I definitely wouldn't pay such a premium to be exposed to these risks.

However, the stock isn't significantly overvalued.

In my view, it's trading just a bit above fair value in the mid-$40s range. Beyond that, it's still the same old boring but steady bond proxy you can lean on in tougher times. Dividends are well covered and should keep coming, so it's a good thing to hold in your portfolio.
